channel.title: NYT > Climate and Environment
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: How Do You Measure Snow From Space? First, Climb a Mountain.
item.description: A new satellite could transform how water is studied worldwide. But to help unlock its capabilities, scientists first needed to take critical measurements on a mountaintop.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Nina Riggio
item.media:credit: Nina Riggio for The New York Times

item.title: Carbon Capture Technology Is Helping This Pub Make Beer
item.description: A pub in California is pulling carbon dioxide from the air to carbonate pints. If the business model works, it could give the broader carbon-capture industry a boost.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Nuwer and Ian C. Bates
item.media:credit: Ian C. Bates for The New York Times

item.title: Trump Administration to Pay $1 Billion to Energy Giant to Cancel Wind Farms
item.description: In exchange, the French company TotalEnergies would invest in oil and natural gas projects in Texas and elsewhere.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Brad Plumer
item.media:credit: Randi Baird for The New York Times
item.media:description: Under the terms, TotalEnergies would forfeit its leases in federal waters for two wind farms that would have been built off New York and North Carolina.

item.title: Maryland’s Supreme Court Strikes Down Baltimore’s Climate Lawsuit
item.description: The decision represents a setback to other local governments around the country that have sued oil companies to recoup the mounting costs of climate change.
item.dc:creator: Karen Zraick
item.media:credit: Stephanie Scarbrough/Associated Press
item.media:description: Crude oil storage in Baltimore. The governments of Baltimore, Annapolis and Anne Arundel County had sued 26 oil and gas companies to recover damages caused by greenhouse gas emissions.

item.title: Life at $4 a Gallon
item.description: The war in Iran has sent gas prices soaring. Here’s what to know about how higher gas prices can change consumer behavior.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Mike Blake/Reuters
item.media:description: A gas station in Los Angeles on March 10.

item.title: Ancient Grapes Reveal Long History of Modern Wines
item.description: Genetic material preserved in ancient grape seeds reveals when, and how, humans meddled with wine grapes.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Dzombak
item.media:credit: Sebastien Bozon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Humans in France were domesticating grapes for making wine by 650 B.C. or earlier, around the time when Greek settlers founded the port city of Marseilles, according to a new study.

item.title: We’ve Been Underestimating Flying Foxes
item.description: Australia used to incinerate the large fruit bats, but research suggests they bring hundreds of millions of dollars in benefits to the country’s economy.
item.dc:creator: Anthony Ham

item.title: How to Reduce the Environmental Impact of Collecting
item.description: From flying commercial to choosing reusable packing crates, art enthusiasts have many options to consider
item.dc:creator: Nina Siegal
item.media:credit: Ben Pearce

item.title: For Western Oil Companies, War in Iran Means Bigger Profits, and Risks
item.description: American and European oil and gas companies are expected to earn a lot more as prices surge but are worried about the future.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: Storage tanks in Stanton, Texas. Companies that operate in places like West Texas are earning a lot more for each barrel of oil they produce.

item.title: U.N. Climate Report Finds Growing Energy Imbalance on Earth
item.description: The continued burning of fossil fuels is locking heat in Earth’s atmosphere, oceans and land — instead of allowing it to reflect back into space, a new report finds.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: JSC/NASA
item.media:description: Greenhouse gases released by humanity have reached their highest level in at least 800,000 years, diminishing the reflectivity of the Earth and its ability to send energy from the sun back into space.

item.title: As Northwest Flooding Worsens, Conflicting Interests Stymie Solutions
item.description: Washington’s floods in December broke records set just four years before, but as farmers, tribes, fishermen and governments search for solutions, a quick fix, dredging, seems to stand in the way.
item.dc:creator: Bernard Mokam and Grant Hindsley

item.title: What to Know About Electric Cars When Gas Prices Are Surging
item.description: War in Iran has disrupted global oil supplies, which is prompting some car shoppers to look for ways to climb off “the gas-price roller coaster.”
item.dc:creator: Jack Ewing
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tristan Spinski for The New York Times
item.media:description: A pre-owned electric vehicle dealership in North Hampton, N.H. There are exceptionally good deals to be had on used battery-powered cars.

item.title: After Cosmic Crisp, Scientists Unveil an Apple for the Climate Change Era
item.description: Agronomists at Washington State University are unveiling a new apple, the Sunflare, which they say tastes as good as their Cosmic Crisp but can withstand unpredictable weather.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin and Grant Hindsley
item.media:credit: Grant Hindsley for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sunflare apples at the Washington State University’s fruit breeding lab in Wenatchee.

item.title: Fearing Chaos of Climate Change, Some Seek Answers in Virtual Classroom
item.description: A monthlong seminar offers lessons in how to anticipate and prepare for the mounting disruptions of global warming.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Mark Davis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alex Steffen, a futurist and former journalist, knew people were hungry for advice on navigating climate change. So he started a class.

item.title: Hochul Moves to Delay and Alter Climate Law, Citing Energy Prices
item.description: The proposal, currently being considered by the Legislature during budget talks, would put off regulations for enforcing the law until 2030.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard
item.category: New York State Energy Research and Development Authority
item.media:credit: Andres Kudacki for The New York Times
item.media:description: In seeking to delay New York State’s Climate Law, Gov. Kathy Hochul cited inflation and tariffs.

item.title: Lee Zeldin, E.P.A. Chief, to Headline Heartland Institute Forum
item.description: Lee Zeldin, the agency administrator, will address a Heartland Institute forum in April. The organization says speakers will challenge the climate crisis “narrative.”
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Elizabeth Frantz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Lee Zeldin, the E.P.A. administrator, in Auburn Hills, Mich., this week.

item.title: How a Power Line From Canada Could Be a New York Lifeline
item.description: The pipeline from Quebec will help New York meet ambitious targets for cutting back on greenhouse gas emissions and could be crucial if the city faces a power shortfall.
item.dc:creator: James Barron
item.media:credit: Clark Hodgin for The New York Times

item.title: Spring Is Here. Do You Care?
item.description: A season celebrated by Shakespeare and the Beatles now ranks behind fall in popularity. After a harsh winter, can it regain its former glory?
item.dc:creator: Steven Kurutz
item.media:credit: Kristian Thacker for The New York Times
item.media:description: Can these little snowdrops compete with maple trees in full autumnal splendor?

item.title: The Planet’s Warning Signs Are Flashing Red
item.description: Climate scientists say many of the effects of climate change are happening faster than they predicted, the latest on the war in Iran and more news.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Matthias Schrader/Associated Press
item.media:description: Sheets to slow melting on the Rhône Glacier near Goms, Switzerland, last year.

item.title: 24 States Sue E.P.A. Over Climate Change Decision
item.description: The suit accuses the agency of illegally repealing the endangerment finding, the scientific assessment that required it to regulate greenhouse gases.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Brian Snyder/Reuters
item.media:description: Andrea Joy Campbell, the attorney general of Massachusetts, which is leading the lawsuit along with California, New York and Connecticut.

item.title: Attacks on Oil and Natural Gas Facilities Could Lead to Much Higher Prices
item.description: Attacks on oil and natural gas facilities this week could make it much harder for Persian Gulf countries to rebuild and restart production when the war eventually ends.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Altaf Qadri/Associated Press
item.media:description: Smoke rose from an oil site in Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, on Saturday. Energy experts say more attacks could cause prices to surge.

item.title: The Weather Is Getting Wilder, and Some See a Dire Signal in the Data
item.description: Several of the Earth’s systems are changing faster than predicted as global temperatures rise, scientists say.
item.dc:creator: David Gelles
item.media:credit: Christinne Muschi/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: A car crushed under a downed tree after strong winds in Montreal, Canada, on Tuesday.

item.title: FEMA Will Relaunch Climate Resiliency Grants
item.description: A judge ruled in December that the agency could not cancel a program that had helped states invest billions of dollars in disaster readiness.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Cliff Owen/Associated Press
item.media:description: Noel Boxer, an external affairs officer with FEMA, inspects the flow of raw sewage near the Potomac River in Cabin John, Md., on March 14, 2026.

item.title: For Iranians, Bombing of Gas Field Worsens Already Dire Energy Crisis
item.description: The South Pars gas field, hit by airstrikes on Wednesday, is central to the energy supply of Iran, which was already suffering blackouts before the war.
item.dc:creator: Sanam Mahoozi and Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Vahid Salemi/Associated Press
item.media:description: The South Pars gas field, which Iran shares with Qatar, in 2019. The field accounts for as much as 70 percent of Iran’s gas production.

item.title: Mullin Signals He Would End Noem Policy That Slowed Disaster Aid
item.description: The president’s nominee, Markwayne Mullin, said he would avoid “micromanaging” FEMA.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Markwayne Mullin, Republican of Oklahoma, at his confirmation hearing for Department of Homeland Security secretary on Wednesday.

item.title: Energy Facilities Attacked in Iran and Qatar, Sending Prices Soaring
item.description: The strikes appeared to be some of the most significant attacks on energy sites since the U.S.-Israeli air war against Iran began almost three weeks ago.
item.dc:creator: Ravi Mattu and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Vahid Salemi/Associated Press
item.media:description: Refineries at the South Pars gas field in 2019.

item.title: The Iran War Is Pushing Some Countries to Cut Back On Energy Use
item.description: As the conflict enters its third week, some nations are trying to reduce energy use, including a mandatory energy holiday in Sri Lanka.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Altaf Qadri/Associated Press
item.media:description: A damaged oil facility in Fujairah, United Arab Emirates, on Saturday.

item.title: Democrats Hammer Trump on ‘Energy Affordability’
item.description: A new Senate report argues the Trump administration has also pushed costs up by stifling clean energy.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Chuck Schumer of New York, the minority leader, right, released the report with Senator Sheldon Whitehouse of Rhode Island, left, the top Democrat on the environment committee, along with Senator Martin Heinrich of New Mexico.

item.title: How a Melting Glacier Could Affect Millions
item.description: As our climate reporter Raymond Zhong wraps up his reporting trip to Antarctica, he reflects on how the collapse of the Thwaites Glacier could redraw coastlines and displace millions of people in some of the world’s fastest-growing cities.
item.dc:creator: Raymond Zhong, Mira Rojanasakul, Chang W. Lee, Jon Miller, Christina Thornell and Stephanie Swart

item.title: Why March Weather Is Weird: Snow, Tornadoes, Recording-Breaking Heat and More
item.description: Snow, tornadoes, record-breaking heat, a dust storm — and that’s just last weekend. Here’s how to understand what’s going on, and the role climate change is playing in all of it.
item.dc:creator: Judson Jones and Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: Richard Tsong-Taatarii/Minnesota Star Tribune, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Minneapolis faced a blizzard over the weekend.

item.title: Trump Officials Weigh New Plan to Stop Offshore Wind Farms
item.description: Proposed settlements would block wind farms off New York State and North Carolina, according to documents reviewed by The New York Times.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.category: Bureau of Ocean Energy Management, Regulation and Enforcement
item.media:credit: Stephane Mahe/Reuters
item.media:description: Under proposed settlements, the Interior Department would cancel the leases for two offshore wind projects, while the Justice Department would pay more than $928 million to the developers to reimburse them for their winning bids in lease sales.

item.title: How To Bring a Bird’s Song Back from the Edge of Extinction
item.description: In a new study, wild regent honeyeaters became vocal tutors, teaching their disappearing song to birds in a captive breeding program.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:credit: RCAustralia/Alamy
item.media:description: The regent honeyeater population of Australia has dropped sharply since the mid-20th century. Today scientists estimate that roughly 250 wild birds live in the fragmented forests across an area about the size of Spain.

item.title: Administration Targeted Climate Lab Over Tina Peters, Lawsuit Says
item.description: The complaint says efforts to dismantle an atmospheric research center are part of a broader political campaign that endangers climate and weather studies.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: Mark Makela/Reuters
item.media:description: The National Center for Atmospheric Research in Boulder, Colo.

item.title: Administration to Convene ‘God Squad’ With Power to Override Environmental Law
item.description: The meeting, planned for this month, will focus on oil and gas drilling in the Gulf of Mexico.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Interior Secretary Doug Burgum called a meeting of the so-called God Squad. It will be the first gathering of the panel in three decades.

item.title: What Displays Get Scrapped at America’s Parks? It Looks Like Anyone’s Guess.
item.description: President Trump ordered officials to remove information deemed disparaging to the United States. A review of government documents shows little guidance and striking inconsistencies.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Hannah Yoon for The New York Times
item.media:description: A display on slavery was removed from the Independence National Historical Park in Philadelphia in February. A judge has since ordered it restored.

item.title: What to Know About the Extreme Heat Forecast in Los Angeles, San Francisco and Phoenix
item.description: Meteorologists are not mincing words in their forecasts for record-high temperatures in California and the desert Southwest.
item.dc:creator: Erin McCann
item.media:credit: Stephen Lam/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: It’s unusual for San Francisco to reach 80 degrees any time of the year, but temperatures are expected to climb even higher all this week.

item.title: Hydropower Line From Quebec to Queens Could Power a Million N.Y.C. Homes
item.description: A 339-mile buried transmission line is on schedule to bring clean electricity to New York City this spring.
item.dc:creator: Hilary Howard
item.media:credit: Clark Hodgin for The New York Times
item.media:description: Part of the Champlain Hudson Power Express converter station in Astoria, Queens.

item.title: In Ski Towns, a Bad Snow Year Is Worsening Wildfire Fears
item.description: A record-breaking snow drought has residents worried about much more than slushy slopes.
item.dc:creator: Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Nina Riggio
item.media:credit: Nina Riggio for The New York Times
item.media:description: A snow-making machine in Nederland, Colo. Some are now aimed at buildings to help protect against the possibility of wildfires.

item.title: Trump Administration Approves Ultra-Deepwater Oil Drilling Plan
item.description: The $5 billion project in the Gulf of Mexico is expected to help produce up to 10 billion barrels of oil by the end of this decade. Critics say it could endanger people and marine life.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Rebecca F. Elliott
item.category: Bureau of Ocean Energy Management, Regulation and Enforcement
item.media:credit: Erin Schaff/The New York Times
item.media:description: An oil platform in the Gulf of Mexico in 2024.

item.title: Why Little Was Done to Head Off Oil’s Strait of Hormuz Problem
item.description: Geography and regional rivalries have prevented Gulf countries from finding a true alternative to the strait, which the war with Iran has effectively shut down.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Vivian Nereim
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Sahar Al Attar/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: A naval vessel sailing this month in the Strait of Hormuz. The waterway remains the only option to export most of the energy produced in the region.

item.title: How My Trip to Antarctica Unlocked a Family Discovery
item.description: An eight-week voyage to the bottom of the Earth helped the photojournalist Chang W. Lee better understand his late father.
item.dc:creator: Chang W. Lee

item.title: E.P.A. Moves to Weaken Limits on Ethylene Oxide
item.description: The gas, ethylene oxide, plays a crucial role in sterilizing medical devices. But long-term exposure is linked to several types of cancer and other ailments.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Gerald Herbert/Associated Press
item.media:description: A chemical and petroleum industrial corridor, a known source of ethylene oxide emissions, in Ascension Parish, La.

item.title: Trump Administration Readies Plans to Dismantle NCAR Research Lab
item.description: Proposals include transferring a supercomputer to the University of Wyoming and shifting a space weather lab to a private company.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: Caine Delacy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The National Center for Atmospheric Research in Boulder, Colo.

item.title: Palisades Fire Recovery Tests L.A.’s Ability to Invest in Resilience
item.description: Palisades fire victims want to raise money for disaster hardening. Their idea could be a model — if it can get past L.A.’s most vexing housing problems.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Stephen Ross Goldstein for The New York Times
item.media:description: Marcela Silva at the site of her former Pacific Palisades home.

item.title: The Largest Oil Supply Disruption in History
item.description: A look at the war in Iran’s effects on global energy markets, two activists whose Sierra Club memberships were revoked, and more climate and environmental news.
item.dc:creator: Claire Brown
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Mohammed Aty/Reuters
item.media:description: A damaged tanker carrying Iraqi fuel oil in waters near Basra, Iraq, on Thursday.

item.title: Trump Administration Sues California Over Tailpipe Emissions Limits
item.description: A lawsuit argues that the state’s regulations would illegally force a rapid transition to electric vehicles.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow and Lisa Friedman
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Trump administration has systematically moved to slash federal support for electric vehicles.

item.title: Odds Rise That El Niño Will Soon Bring Weather Extremes
item.description: The climate pattern known for intense heat, floods and drought is likely to develop this summer, raising questions about disaster preparedness.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: A flooded street in Cathedral City, Calif., in 2023. Scientists have linked the 2023-24 El Niño to drought in Southern Africa, large wildfires in the Amazon and intense rainfall in Southern California.

item.title: Brian Joondeph’s Appointment to Air Pollution Advisory Board Draws Pushback
item.description: Brian Joondeph, a Colorado-based ophthalmologist and political commentator, has not published peer-reviewed research on air pollution science.
item.dc:creator: Maxine Joselow
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: By law, the Clean Air Scientific Advisory Committee of the E.P.A. must include a physician, usually a pulmonologist or cardiologist with expertise in how exposure to air pollution increases the risk of asthma, heart and lung diseases, cancer and other ailments.

item.title: Her Lab Worked to Future-Proof Fruits and Vegetables
item.description: Erin McGuire ran a research network that studied how to get healthy food to marginalized populations around the world.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.category: United States Agency for International Development
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: William Keo for The New York Times

item.title: Iran Shocks Could Spur a Shift to Clean Energy — But Also to Coal
item.description: The oil crisis in the Middle East could spur countries to invest in wind, solar and other renewables. It could also spike reliance on coal, a cheap and polluting fossil fuel.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Lisa Friedman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Majid Saeedi/Getty Images
item.media:description: A plume of smoke from Israeli-U. S. airstrikes on Tehran on March 2.
